common_name: Adirondack Blue Potato (Cornell High-Anthocyanin Blue Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; Adirondack Blue was released in 2003 by Cornell University's potato breeding program, specifically developed as a distinct improvement over older All Blue-type varieties with higher anthocyanin content, better yield, improved disease resistance, and more consistent blue-purple flesh coloration throughout the tuber without white streaking.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 10–12 inches apart in rows 30–36 inches apart, 3–4 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost in cool weather; hill soil over emerging plants twice as they grow to increase yield and prevent tubers from greening. Adirondack Blue was developed by Dr. Walter De Jong and colleagues at Cornell University's potato breeding program and released in 2003, representing a significant improvement over older blue-flesh potato varieties; it was specifically bred for the uniformly intense blue-purple flesh coloration (without the white marbling or streaking common in older blue varieties), higher anthocyanin content (the pigment responsible for blue and purple coloration and associated health benefits), and better agronomic performance than heirloom blue types. The tubers have blue-purple skin and uniformly vibrant blue-purple flesh that holds its color remarkably well when boiled or steamed; the texture is waxy and the flavor is rich and earthy — similar to high-quality waxy potatoes with an additional visual dimension. The blue color is striking in potato salads, mashed potatoes (turns lavender-blue), and roasted presentations. Mid-season maturity of 70–90 days from planting; produces reliably in most US climates.

common_name: Adirondack Red Potato (Cornell Red-Flesh Specialty Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; Adirondack Red was released in 2004 by Cornell University's potato breeding program as a companion to Adirondack Blue, producing uniformly red-pink flesh from peel to center with high anthocyanin content and excellent flavor — the color holds well through cooking and provides nutritional and visual value comparable to the blue version.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 10–12 inches apart, 3–4 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost; hill twice as plants grow. Adirondack Red was released in 2004 by Dr. Walter De Jong's Cornell University potato breeding program, one year after Adirondack Blue, specifically developed to provide a red-fleshed complement to the blue-fleshed Adirondack Blue; both share the goal of combining high anthocyanin content, superior visual appeal, and reliable agronomic performance in a named, certified-disease-free variety. The medium-sized, round to oblong tubers have rose-red skin and uniformly pink to red flesh throughout — significantly more uniformly red than older red-flesh varieties like 'Red Gold' which tend to develop red only near the skin. The flavor is excellent — rich, waxy, earthy, and slightly nutty — with flesh that holds its color when boiled or steamed, making it excellent for colorful potato salads, roasted potato platters, and any presentation where the red color adds visual value. Disease resistance, yield, and overall agronomic performance are significantly improved compared to heirloom red-flesh types.

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; German Butterball is a German heirloom potato renowned as one of the most richly flavored and richly colored yellow-flesh potatoes available — the name accurately describes the extraordinary buttery, creamy flavor and golden-yellow flesh that makes it the benchmark yellow potato for flavor among heirloom enthusiasts.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 10–12 inches apart in rows 30 inches apart, 3–4 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost; German Butterball is a late-mid-season variety (70–90 days) that produces medium-large, round to slightly oval, netted-skin, golden-fleshed tubers. German Butterball is a traditional German heirloom potato that entered American heirloom potato circles through the Rodale Institute and seed saver networks in the 1980s; it is consistently rated among the finest-flavored potato varieties by tasting panels — the rich, buttery, dense golden-yellow flesh has a flavor intensity that significantly exceeds Yukon Gold (the most popular yellow potato commercially) and makes German Butterball a benchmark for those seeking the very best potato flavor. The tubers have thin, netted tan-gold skin and deep golden-yellow flesh with a texture that is semi-waxy when young and becomes more floury when mature, making it excellent for all cooking purposes: baked, roasted, mashed, boiled, or fried. The exceptional flavor when simply roasted or baked with olive oil is what defines German Butterball's reputation — no other potato consistently delivers this depth of rich, buttery potato flavor without any embellishment. One of the most sought-after varieties in the specialty potato market.

common_name: Katahdin Potato (1932 USDA Classic White Storage Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; Katahdin was released in 1932 by the USDA Potato Breeding Program and named for Mount Katahdin in Maine — for much of the 20th century it was the most widely grown potato in the eastern United States, prized for good yields, excellent storage quality, and versatile white flesh suitable for all cooking purposes.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 10–12 inches apart in rows 30–36 inches apart, 3–4 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost; Katahdin is a main-season variety producing large, round to oval, smooth, buff-white-skinned, white-fleshed tubers of excellent quality. Katahdin was developed by the USDA Potato Research Laboratory in Presque Isle, Maine and officially released in 1932, named for the highest peak in Maine; for the next several decades it was the most widely grown potato in the northeastern and mid-Atlantic United States, valued for its consistent productivity, reliable storage quality, and disease resistance (including moderate resistance to late blight). The large, smooth, nearly round tubers have thin, buff to light tan skin and white flesh with a slightly waxy texture when young — excellent for boiling and potato salad — that transitions toward semi-floury when fully mature, making them good for baking and mashing as well; a genuinely all-purpose variety in the tradition of the great white American storage potatoes. Flavor is clean, mild, and characteristic of the best white potato types; stores 4–6 months under proper conditions (35–40°F, high humidity). Still grown commercially in the Northeast and available from heritage potato suppliers.

common_name: Purple Peruvian Fingerling Potato (Andean Purple Heritage Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; Purple Peruvian is an Andean heirloom fingerling potato originating in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia where hundreds of diverse potato varieties developed over 8,000+ years of indigenous cultivation — it produces long, slender, finger-shaped tubers with deep purple skin and deep purple flesh, representing one of the many colorful heritage potato types from the center of potato genetic diversity.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces (whole small tubers work best for fingerlings) 10–12 inches apart, 3–4 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost; fingerling potatoes produce many small, elongated tubers that benefit from slightly closer planting than large-tuber varieties. Purple Peruvian fingerling potatoes come from the Andean highlands of Peru and Bolivia, the center of origin of the domestic potato (Solanum tuberosum), where indigenous Quechua and Aymara peoples developed thousands of diverse potato cultivars over 8,000+ years — the wild ancestral diversity of potatoes in this region dwarfs the few dozen varieties commonly grown in North America. Purple Peruvian produces long (2–4 inch), slender, finger-shaped tubers with intensely dark purple to near-black skin and uniformly deep purple to violet flesh throughout; the color comes from the same anthocyanin pigments found in Adirondack Blue and All Blue but at high concentrations. The texture is waxy and the flavor is earthy, slightly nutty, and distinctly more complex than commercial potato varieties — reflecting centuries of selection in high-altitude Andean environments. The fingerling shape makes them visually dramatic on the plate and ideal for roasting whole (the skin caramelizes beautifully), potato salads, and presentations where the purple color adds visual impact. High in antioxidants.
